pr.title: Update `publish.yml` to deploy progress site [skip editorconfig]
pr.author: ribbanya

summary: Unmerged PR that attempted to deploy a progress site through the GitHub Pages publish workflow. It added a Dockerfile-backed CI environment, replaced repeated compiler/WiBo setup in GitHub Actions with a prebuilt `ribbanya/melee:20230219` container, integrated a Svelte progress site under `tools/progress-site/site`, and added a Python progress calculator under `tools/progress-site/calc`. The PR body says it depended on #740 and imported old progress site repositories as subtrees, with an expectation that they would later be refactored or phased out as automated data updates improved.
what_changed: Changed `.github/workflows/build.yml` to run inside `ribbanya/melee:20230219` instead of `devkitpro/devkitppc:latest`, removing in-workflow setup for devkitPro packages, WiBo checkout/build, and compiler download/patching.
Updated the build workflow to symlink `/usr/local/bin/mwcc_compiler` into `tools/` and run `make` using `WINE=$WINE`, relying on the container image to provide WiBo and compiler tooling.
Updated the build workflow artifact upload action from `actions/upload-artifact@v2` to `actions/upload-artifact@v3`.

Changed `.github/workflows/publish.yml` to run inside the same prebuilt container and simplified Doxygen generation by creating `build/doxygen`, running `doxygen Doxyfile`, and touching `.nojekyll` in one shell step.
Removed the publish workflow's apt package cache step for `doxygen graphviz`, implying those dependencies were expected to come from the container.

Kept Doxygen Awesome checkout and added a `Generate context` step that activates `/usr/local/share/venv`, installs Python requirements without cache, runs `tools/m2ctx/m2ctx.py -pqr`, and copies `build/ctx.html` into the generated docs.

Added a `Build progress site` step in `publish.yml` that runs `npm install` and `npm run build` in `tools/progress-site/site`, then copies built public assets into `build/doxygen/html/progress` for GitHub Pages deployment.

Added a root `Dockerfile` that builds a CI image from `devkitpro/devkitppc:20230110`, upgrades through Debian releases, installs build, Python, Node, npm, and multilib dependencies, builds WiBo, fetches and patches the GC compiler package, creates a `mwcceppc` wrapper, and preinstalls Python requirements in a venv.

Added a Python progress calculator subtree under `tools/progress-site/calc`, including `calcprogress.py`, `main.py`, `patch.py`, a sample/generated `result.csv`, README, `.gitignore`, and IDE metadata.
Added a Svelte/Rollup progress site subtree under `tools/progress-site/site`, including package metadata, lockfiles, app components, CSS, TypeScript config, README, and GitHub Pages helper script.

The calculator reads `baserom.dol` section sizes and `tempRepo/build/ssbm.us.1.2/GALE01.map`, parses Metrowerks map symbols with GC/Wii regex patterns, classifies text/data sections, and computes code/data completion percentages plus trophy/event count analogies.
The PR was closed without merge, so these exact workflow and subtree additions were not accepted in this PR as recorded.
smart_moves: Moving compiler, WiBo, Node, Doxygen, and Python setup into a container would make CI and Pages generation faster and more reproducible than repeatedly downloading and building dependencies in each workflow run.
Using the same container image for both build and publish workflows reduces environment drift between map generation, docs generation, context generation, and progress-site deployment.
Deploying the progress site as a subdirectory of the generated Doxygen HTML tree lets GitHub Pages publish documentation and progress reporting through one artifact upload.
Creating `.nojekyll` during Doxygen generation preserves GitHub Pages compatibility for documentation paths containing underscores.
The progress calculator separates code and data completion by reading DOL section sizes and parsing map file section/object data, which is more meaningful than counting files or symbols alone.
The PR body explicitly identifies the progress-site code as imported old repos/subtrees and marks them for later refactor or phase-out, which is useful scope-setting for a large tooling import.
decomp_lessons: For decompilation CI, caching or containerizing nontrivial toolchain setup can substantially simplify workflow YAML and reduce reliance on repeated external downloads during each run.
A progress site can be integrated with the existing Doxygen Pages deployment by building frontend assets into a dedicated subdirectory such as `build/doxygen/html/progress`.
Progress calculation should be based on the linker map and DOL section sizes when possible, because these reflect actual matched/decompiled bytes more directly than source-file counts.
When parsing Metrowerks map files, scripts need to handle section-layout lines, text/data section classification, filler symbols, file-wide section symbols, `UNUSED` markers, and `entry of` lines.
Subtree imports of old tooling can be useful for preserving functionality quickly, but the PR itself notes that such imports should later be refactored or phased out as automation improves.
Committing IDE metadata and both npm and yarn lockfiles can add substantial noise to a tooling PR; future imports should consider trimming editor-specific files unless intentionally required.
naming_conventions: Progress tooling was placed under `tools/progress-site/` with separate `calc` and `site` subdirectories, clearly splitting data generation from frontend presentation.
Workflow step names used descriptive action phrases such as `Generate Doxygen documentation`, `Generate context`, and `Build progress site`.
The Python calculator used explicit section/category names such as `TEXT_SECTIONS`, `DATA_SECTIONS`, `TEXT_SECTION_COUNT`, `DATA_SECTION_COUNT`, `SECTION_TEXT`, and `SECTION_DATA`.
The progress data model used names such as `codeCompletionPcnt`, `dataCompletionPcnt`, `trophyCount`, and `eventCount`, reflecting the displayed progress metrics.
The Dockerfile used staged names that document setup phases: `buster`, `bullseye`, `bookworm`, `install-packages`, `setup-wibo`, `setup-melee`, `setup-mwcc`, and `setup-venv`.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: No direct C/assembly matching changes were present.
The tooling's matching-related tactic was to estimate project progress from `GALE01.map` and `baserom.dol` by summing decompiled text/data symbol sizes against DOL section sizes.

The calculator ignored symbols that appeared to be ASM or unparseable by the selected Metrowerks GC map regex, skipped `UNUSED` entries, skipped filler and section-wide symbols, and used object continuity checks before accumulating symbol sizes.
The build workflow preserved map generation via the `GENERATE_MAP=1` matrix and uploaded `build/ssbm.us.1.2/GALE01.map`, keeping linker maps available for downstream progress analysis.
review_feedback: No issue comments, review comments, or reviews were present in the provided PR slice.
The PR was closed unmerged; the slice does not provide reviewer rationale for closure.
The PR body states it depended on #740 and that old progress site repos were added as subtrees to be refactored or phased out, but this is author context rather than review feedback.
follow_up_queries: Find the eventual merged PR that added or replaced the progress site deployment after PR #741 was closed.
Inspect PR #740 to understand the Docker/container dependency referenced by this PR.
Search for later changes to `.github/workflows/publish.yml` involving `tools/progress-site`, `progress`, or GitHub Pages.
Search for later removals or refactors of `tools/progress-site/calc` and `tools/progress-site/site` subtree files.
Compare later progress calculation scripts against `calcprogress.py` to see which map parsing tactics were retained.
